parentQuestion: Ptosis, dilated pupil, eye down and out

scaffolds.explain: The muscle that lifts your upper eyelid is controlled by CN III.

scaffolds.q: Pupils normally get smaller in bright light.
scaffolds.explain: A dilated pupil suggests the nerve controlling constriction is damaged.

scaffolds.q: CN III controls the muscle that constricts the pupil.
scaffolds.explain: CN III carries parasympathetic fibers to sphincter pupillae.

scaffolds.q: Down and out means eye looks downward and outward.
scaffolds.explain: When CN III fails, remaining muscles pull eye down and out.

scaffolds.q: CN III controls 4 of the 6 eye movement muscles.
scaffolds.explain: When CN III fails, remaining muscles (CN IV, VI) pull unopposed.

scaffolds.q: Multiple symptoms affecting one eye suggest one nerve controls all.
scaffolds.explain: Ptosis + dilated pupil + eye deviation = classic CN III palsy.

scaffolds.q: The oculomotor nerve has multiple jobs: eyelid, pupil, eye movement.
scaffolds.explain: CN III is a busy nerve with several functions.

scaffolds.q: CN III palsy causes ptosis, mydriasis, and eye deviation.
scaffolds.explain: Classic triad of oculomotor nerve damage.

scaffolds.q: Third nerve palsy can be caused by aneurysm compression.
scaffolds.explain: Posterior communicating artery aneurysm can compress CN III.
